science.title: Lesson 1: The Human Body Systems
science.description: Learn about the amazing systems that keep your body working.

topics.name: Skeletal System
topics.description: Learn about the bones that give your body shape and support.

content.introduction: Your skeletal system is the strong frame inside your body. It is made of 206 bones in adults. Bones support your body, give it shape, and protect important organs.

sections.title: What Bones Do
sections.body: Bones give the body shape and support so you can stand and move. They also protect soft organs inside you.

sections.title: Important Bones
sections.body: The skull protects the brain, the rib cage protects the heart and lungs, and the spine supports your back and keeps you upright.

sections.title: Inside a Bone
sections.body: Many bones have a soft part called bone marrow inside them. Bone marrow makes blood cells for the body.

definition.meaning: The soft tissue inside many bones that makes blood cells.

content.keyTakeaway: The skeletal system has 206 bones that support and protect the body.
content.importantNote: Bones are living tissue, so eating healthy food helps keep them strong.

quiz.question: What is found inside many bones that makes blood cells?
quiz.explanation: Bone marrow is the soft tissue inside bones that makes blood cells.

quiz.question: How many bones does the adult human body have?
quiz.explanation: The adult human body has 206 bones.

quiz.question: Which body part protects the brain?
quiz.explanation: The skull is the bony case that protects the brain.

quiz.question: What is the main job of the rib cage?
options: To protect the heart and lungs
quiz.explanation: The rib cage surrounds and protects the heart and lungs.

quiz.question: Which part of the skeleton helps you stand up straight and supports your back?
quiz.explanation: The spine, or backbone, supports the body and keeps you upright.

quiz.question: Which is an example of a job done by the skeletal system?
options: Giving the body shape and support
quiz.explanation: The skeletal system gives the body shape, support, and protection.

quiz.question: The spine is made of small bones called what?
quiz.explanation: The spine is made of small bones called vertebrae.

quiz.question: Which compares the skull and the rib cage correctly?
quiz.explanation: Both the skull and rib cage are bony parts that protect important organs.

quiz.question: What is the term for the hard, living tissue that bones are made of?
quiz.explanation: Bone is the hard, living tissue that forms the skeleton.

quiz.question: Without the skeletal system, what would happen to the body?
options: It would have no shape and could not stand
quiz.explanation: Without bones, the body would have no support and could not hold its shape.

topics.name: Muscular System
topics.description: Discover how muscles help your body move.

content.introduction: Muscles are special tissues that help your body move. They pull on bones to make you walk, run, and lift things. Some muscles you control, and some work on their own.

sections.title: How Muscles Move You
sections.body: Muscles connect to bones using strong bands called tendons. Muscles pull on bones to make them move.

sections.title: Two Kinds of Muscles
sections.body: Voluntary muscles are the ones you control, like your arm muscles. Involuntary muscles work on their own, like the muscles in your stomach.

sections.title: Working in Pairs
sections.body: Muscles often work in pairs. The bicep and tricep in your arm take turns. When one tightens, the other relaxes. The heart has a special cardiac muscle.

definition.meaning: A strong band that connects a muscle to a bone.

content.keyTakeaway: Muscles pull on bones to move the body, and they often work in pairs.
content.importantNote: Stretching before play helps keep your muscles healthy and safe.

quiz.question: What connects muscles to bones?
quiz.explanation: Tendons are strong bands that connect muscles to bones.

quiz.question: Which type of muscle can you control on purpose, like moving your arm?
quiz.explanation: Voluntary muscles are the ones you can move when you choose to.

quiz.question: Which muscle works without you thinking about it, like in your stomach?
quiz.explanation: Involuntary muscles work automatically without you having to think.

quiz.question: The bicep and tricep work together as a pair. When the bicep tightens, the tricep does what?
quiz.explanation: Muscles work in pairs; when the bicep tightens, the tricep relaxes.

quiz.question: Which special muscle is found only in the heart?
quiz.explanation: Cardiac muscle is the special muscle found only in the heart.

quiz.question: What is the main job of the muscular system?
quiz.explanation: The muscular system helps the body move by pulling on bones.

quiz.question: Which is an example of a voluntary muscle action?
quiz.explanation: Kicking a ball is a voluntary action you choose to do.

quiz.question: How do muscles move bones?
quiz.explanation: Muscles can only pull, not push, so they pull on bones to move them.

quiz.question: Which correctly compares voluntary and involuntary muscles?
options: You control voluntary; involuntary work on their own
You control involuntary; voluntary work on their own
Both are only in the heart
quiz.explanation: Voluntary muscles are controlled by you; involuntary muscles work automatically.

quiz.question: What is the term for the band that joins muscle to bone?
quiz.explanation: A tendon is the band that joins a muscle to a bone.

topics.name: Digestive System
topics.description: Follow the path of food through your body.

content.introduction: The digestive system breaks down food so your body can use it for energy. Food travels through many parts, starting in the mouth.

sections.title: It Starts in the Mouth
sections.body: Digestion begins in the mouth. Teeth chew food into small pieces, and saliva helps break it down.

sections.title: Down to the Stomach
sections.body: Food slides down a tube called the esophagus to the stomach. The stomach mixes food with strong juices.

sections.title: The Intestines
sections.body: In the small intestine, tiny finger-like villi absorb nutrients into the blood. The large intestine absorbs water and forms waste.

definition.meaning: Tiny finger-like parts in the small intestine that absorb nutrients.

content.keyTakeaway: Food travels from the mouth to the stomach and intestines, where nutrients are absorbed.
content.importantNote: Eating slowly and chewing well helps your digestive system work better.

quiz.question: Where does digestion begin?
quiz.explanation: Digestion begins in the mouth where teeth and saliva start breaking down food.

quiz.question: What does saliva do to food in the mouth?
quiz.explanation: Saliva helps break down food so it is easier to swallow and digest.

quiz.question: Which tube carries food from the mouth to the stomach?
quiz.explanation: The esophagus is the tube that carries food to the stomach.

quiz.question: Where is food mixed with strong juices to break it down further?
quiz.explanation: The stomach mixes food with strong digestive juices.

quiz.question: In the small intestine, tiny finger-like parts called villi do what?
quiz.explanation: Villi are tiny parts in the small intestine that absorb nutrients into the blood.

quiz.question: What is the main job of the large intestine?
options: To absorb water and form waste
quiz.explanation: The large intestine absorbs water and forms solid waste.

quiz.question: What is the term for the tiny finger-like parts in the small intestine?
quiz.explanation: Villi are the tiny finger-like parts that absorb nutrients.

quiz.question: Which is the correct order of these digestive parts?
quiz.explanation: Food goes mouth, then esophagus, then stomach, then small intestine.

quiz.question: Which compares the small and large intestine correctly?
options: Small intestine absorbs nutrients; large intestine absorbs water
Large intestine makes saliva; small intestine breathes
quiz.explanation: The small intestine absorbs nutrients while the large intestine absorbs water.

quiz.question: What is the role of teeth in digestion?
options: To chew and break food into smaller pieces
quiz.explanation: Teeth chew food and break it into smaller pieces.

topics.name: Respiratory System
topics.description: Learn how your body takes in air.

content.introduction: The respiratory system helps you breathe. It takes in oxygen, which your body needs, and removes carbon dioxide, which your body does not need.

sections.title: Air Comes In
sections.body: Air usually enters through the nose, which warms and cleans it. Then it goes down the trachea, or windpipe.

sections.title: Into the Lungs
sections.body: The trachea splits into two tubes called bronchi, one for each lung. Inside the lungs are tiny air sacs called alveoli.

sections.title: The Breathing Muscle
sections.body: Oxygen passes from the alveoli into the blood. A muscle called the diaphragm moves to help you breathe in and out.

definition.meaning: Tiny air sacs in the lungs where oxygen passes into the blood.

content.keyTakeaway: Air travels from the nose to the alveoli, where oxygen enters the blood.
content.importantNote: Breathing fresh, clean air keeps your lungs healthy.

quiz.question: Through which body part does air usually enter the body?
quiz.explanation: Air usually enters the body through the nose, which warms and cleans it.

quiz.question: What is the windpipe also called?
quiz.explanation: The trachea is the windpipe that carries air toward the lungs.

quiz.question: The trachea splits into two tubes called what?
quiz.explanation: The trachea splits into two tubes called bronchi, one for each lung.

quiz.question: What are the tiny air sacs in the lungs called?
quiz.explanation: Alveoli are the tiny air sacs where oxygen enters the blood.

quiz.question: Which muscle helps the lungs by moving up and down when you breathe?
quiz.explanation: The diaphragm is the muscle below the lungs that helps you breathe.

quiz.question: What is the main job of the respiratory system?
options: To take in oxygen and remove carbon dioxide
quiz.explanation: The respiratory system takes in oxygen and removes carbon dioxide.

quiz.question: Where does oxygen pass into the blood?
quiz.explanation: Oxygen passes into the blood through the thin walls of the alveoli.

quiz.question: Which is the correct path of air into the lungs?
quiz.explanation: Air travels from the nose to the trachea, then bronchi, then alveoli.

quiz.question: Which compares the trachea and bronchi correctly?
options: The trachea is one tube; the bronchi are two tubes
The bronchi are above the nose
quiz.explanation: The trachea is a single tube that splits into the two bronchi.

quiz.question: What is the role of the nose in breathing?
options: To warm, clean, and let in air
quiz.explanation: The nose warms, cleans, and lets air into the body.

topics.name: Circulatory System
topics.description: See how blood moves around your body.

content.introduction: The circulatory system moves blood around your body. Blood carries oxygen, nutrients, and other important things to keep you alive. The heart is the pump.

sections.title: The Heart
sections.body: The heart has four chambers. It pumps blood through the whole body without stopping.

sections.title: Blood Vessels
sections.body: Arteries carry blood away from the heart, veins carry it back, and tiny capillaries connect them.

sections.title: Parts of Blood
sections.body: Red blood cells carry oxygen, white blood cells fight germs, platelets help clot cuts, and plasma is the liquid part of blood.

definition.meaning: Tiny blood vessels that connect arteries and veins.

content.keyTakeaway: The heart pumps blood through arteries, veins, and capillaries to keep the body alive.
content.importantNote: Exercise helps keep your heart strong and healthy.

quiz.question: How many chambers does the human heart have?
quiz.explanation: The human heart has four chambers.

quiz.question: Which blood vessels carry blood away from the heart?
quiz.explanation: Arteries carry blood away from the heart.

quiz.question: Which tiny blood vessels connect arteries and veins?
quiz.explanation: Capillaries are the tiny vessels that connect arteries and veins.

quiz.question: What is the main job of red blood cells?
quiz.explanation: Red blood cells carry oxygen around the body.

quiz.question: Which blood cells help fight germs and sickness?
quiz.explanation: White blood cells help fight germs and keep us from getting sick.

quiz.question: What do platelets do?
options: Help blood clot to stop bleeding
quiz.explanation: Platelets help the blood clot so cuts stop bleeding.

quiz.question: What is the liquid part of blood called?
quiz.explanation: Plasma is the liquid part of blood that carries cells and nutrients.

quiz.question: Which compares arteries and veins correctly?
options: Arteries carry blood away from the heart; veins carry blood back
Veins carry blood away; arteries carry blood back
Both carry blood only to the brain
quiz.explanation: Arteries carry blood away from the heart while veins carry it back.

quiz.question: What is the main job of the circulatory system?
options: To move blood, oxygen, and nutrients around the body
quiz.explanation: The circulatory system moves blood, oxygen, and nutrients around the body.

quiz.question: Which is an example of a blood part that fights sickness?
quiz.explanation: White blood cells fight sickness and germs.

summative.question: What is found inside many bones that makes blood cells?
summative.explanation: Bone marrow is the soft tissue inside bones that makes blood cells.

summative.explanation: The adult human body has 206 bones.

science.title: Lesson 2: Matter and Its Properties
science.description: Explore what everything around us is made of.

topics.name: States of Matter
topics.description: Explore the three main states of matter.

content.introduction: Matter is anything that takes up space and has mass. Matter comes in three main states: solid, liquid, and gas. The way tiny molecules move makes each state different.

sections.title: Solid, Liquid, Gas
sections.body: A solid keeps its shape. A liquid takes the shape of its container. A gas spreads out to fill the space.

sections.title: How Molecules Behave
sections.body: In a solid, molecules are packed tightly. In a liquid, they move more freely. In a gas, they move fast and spread out.

sections.title: Changing States
sections.body: When a gas becomes a liquid, it is called condensation. When a liquid becomes a gas, it is called evaporation.

definition.meaning: A tiny particle that makes up matter.

content.keyTakeaway: Matter has three main states: solid, liquid, and gas, based on how molecules move.
content.importantNote: Water is special because we can see it as a solid (ice), liquid, and gas (steam).

quiz.question: How many main states of matter are there?
quiz.explanation: There are three main states of matter: solid, liquid, and gas.

quiz.question: Which state of matter keeps its own shape?
quiz.explanation: A solid keeps its own shape because its molecules are packed tightly.

quiz.question: Which state of matter takes the shape of its container but keeps its volume?
quiz.explanation: A liquid takes the shape of its container but keeps the same volume.

quiz.question: In a gas, how do the molecules behave?
options: They move fast and spread out
quiz.explanation: In a gas, molecules move fast and spread out to fill the space.

quiz.question: What is it called when a gas changes into a liquid?
quiz.explanation: Condensation is when a gas changes into a liquid.

quiz.question: What is it called when a liquid changes into a gas?
quiz.explanation: Evaporation is when a liquid changes into a gas.

quiz.question: Which is an example of a solid?
quiz.explanation: A rock is a solid because it keeps its own shape.

quiz.question: Which compares solids and gases correctly?
options: Solid molecules are tightly packed; gas molecules spread out
Both have molecules that cannot move
Gas keeps its shape; solid spreads out
quiz.explanation: Solid molecules are packed tightly while gas molecules spread out.

quiz.question: Water droplets forming on a cold glass is an example of what?
quiz.explanation: Droplets on a cold glass form by condensation as gas turns to liquid.

quiz.question: What is the term for the tiny particles that make up matter?
quiz.explanation: Molecules are the tiny particles that make up all matter.

topics.name: Physical Properties
topics.description: Learn the properties we can observe in matter.

content.introduction: Every kind of matter has physical properties we can observe or measure without changing it into something new. These help us describe and compare materials.

sections.title: Mass and Volume
sections.body: Mass is the amount of matter in an object. Volume is the amount of space it takes up.

sections.title: Density and Hardness
sections.body: Density compares mass to volume. Hardness tells how easily something can be scratched.

sections.title: Magnetism and Solubility
sections.body: Magnetism is when a magnet can pull some metals. Solubility is how well something dissolves in a liquid.

definition.meaning: How much mass is packed into a certain volume.

content.keyTakeaway: Physical properties like mass, volume, density, and solubility help us describe matter.
content.importantNote: We can observe physical properties without changing the material into something new.

quiz.question: What is the amount of matter in an object called?
quiz.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter in an object.

quiz.question: What is the amount of space an object takes up called?
quiz.explanation: Volume is the amount of space an object takes up.

quiz.question: Density compares an object's mass to its what?
quiz.explanation: Density compares how much mass is packed into a certain volume.

quiz.question: Which property tells how easily an object can be scratched?
quiz.explanation: Hardness tells how easily an object can be scratched or dented.

quiz.question: Which property lets some metals be pulled by a magnet?
quiz.explanation: Magnetism is the property that lets some metals be pulled by a magnet.

quiz.question: What is solubility?
options: How well something dissolves in a liquid
quiz.explanation: Solubility is how well a material dissolves in a liquid.

quiz.question: Which is an example of using magnetism?
quiz.explanation: A magnet picking up nails is an example of magnetism.

quiz.question: Which compares mass and volume correctly?
options: Mass is the amount of matter; volume is the space it takes up
Mass is space; volume is matter
quiz.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter while volume is the space it takes up.

quiz.question: Sugar dissolving in water is an example of which property?
quiz.explanation: Sugar dissolving in water shows solubility.

quiz.question: A physical property is something you can observe without doing what?
options: Changing it into a new substance
quiz.explanation: A physical property can be observed without changing the material into something new.

topics.name: Changes in Matter
topics.description: Discover how matter can change.

content.introduction: Matter can change in different ways. Some changes only change the look or shape, while others make a brand new substance.

sections.title: Physical and Chemical
sections.body: A physical change does not make a new substance, like cutting paper. A chemical change makes a new substance, like burning wood.

sections.title: Reversible and Irreversible
sections.body: A reversible change can be undone, like melting ice. An irreversible change cannot be easily undone, like baking a cake.

sections.title: Clues of Chemical Change
sections.body: Signs of a chemical change include a new color, a new smell, bubbles of gas, or heat being made.

definition.meaning: A change that makes a brand new substance.

content.keyTakeaway: Physical changes keep the same substance, but chemical changes make new ones.
content.importantNote: Burning, rusting, and food spoiling are all chemical changes.

quiz.question: A change that does not make a new substance is called what?
quiz.explanation: A physical change does not make a new substance.

quiz.question: A change that makes a brand new substance is called what?
quiz.explanation: A chemical change makes a brand new substance.

quiz.question: Which is an example of a physical change?
quiz.explanation: Cutting paper is a physical change because no new substance forms.

quiz.question: Which is an example of a chemical change?
quiz.explanation: Burning wood is a chemical change because new substances form.

quiz.question: A change that can be undone is called what?
quiz.explanation: A reversible change can be undone, like melting and freezing water.

quiz.question: A change that cannot be easily undone is called what?
quiz.explanation: An irreversible change cannot be easily undone, like burning.

quiz.question: Which is a clue that a chemical change happened?
options: A new color, smell, gas, or heat appears
quiz.explanation: New color, smell, gas, or heat are clues of a chemical change.

quiz.question: Which compares physical and chemical changes correctly?
options: Physical change keeps the same substance; chemical change makes a new one
Physical change makes a new substance; chemical change keeps the same
Both always make a new substance
quiz.explanation: A physical change keeps the same substance; a chemical change makes a new one.

quiz.question: Melting ice into water is which kind of change?
quiz.explanation: Melting ice is a physical change that can be reversed by freezing.

quiz.question: Which is an example of an irreversible change?
quiz.explanation: Baking a cake is irreversible; you cannot turn it back into batter.

topics.name: Mixtures and Solutions
topics.description: Learn how materials combine and separate.

content.introduction: When we put materials together, we can make mixtures and solutions. We can also separate them again using different methods.

sections.title: Mixtures and Solutions
sections.body: A mixture is two or more materials combined. A solution is a special mixture where one material fully dissolves in another.

sections.title: Solute and Solvent
sections.body: In a solution, the solute is the part that dissolves, and the solvent is the part that does the dissolving, like salt and water.

sections.title: Separating Materials
sections.body: We can separate mixtures by filtering, using a magnet, or by evaporation to remove a liquid.

definition.meaning: A mixture where one material fully dissolves in another.

content.keyTakeaway: A solution is a mixture where one material dissolves fully into another.
content.importantNote: Salt water is a solution: salt is the solute and water is the solvent.

quiz.question: What is formed when two or more materials are combined but not joined chemically?
quiz.explanation: A mixture is two or more materials combined without a chemical change.

quiz.question: What is a solution?
options: A mixture where one material fully dissolves in another
quiz.explanation: A solution is a mixture where one material fully dissolves in another.

quiz.question: In salt water, what is the solute?
quiz.explanation: In salt water, the salt is the solute because it dissolves.

quiz.question: In salt water, what is the solvent?
quiz.explanation: In salt water, the water is the solvent that dissolves the salt.

quiz.question: Which method separates a solid from a liquid using paper or cloth?
quiz.explanation: Filtering separates a solid from a liquid using paper or cloth.

quiz.question: Which method can separate salt from salt water?
quiz.explanation: Evaporation removes the water and leaves the salt behind.

quiz.question: Which is an example of a mixture?
options: A single drop of pure water
quiz.explanation: A bowl of fruit salad is a mixture of different foods.

quiz.question: Which compares a solute and a solvent correctly?
options: The solute dissolves; the solvent does the dissolving
The solvent dissolves; the solute does the dissolving
quiz.explanation: The solute is the part that dissolves; the solvent does the dissolving.

quiz.question: Which method would separate iron bits from sand?
quiz.explanation: A magnet can pull out iron bits from sand.

quiz.question: Which compares a mixture and a solution correctly?
options: In a solution the material fully dissolves; in many mixtures it does not
quiz.explanation: In a solution the material fully dissolves, while in many mixtures parts can still be seen.

summative.question: How many main states of matter are there?
summative.explanation: There are three main states of matter: solid, liquid, and gas.

summative.question: Which state of matter keeps its own shape?
summative.explanation: A solid keeps its own shape because its molecules are packed tightly.

summative.question: Which state of matter takes the shape of its container but keeps its volume?
summative.explanation: A liquid takes the shape of its container but keeps the same volume.

summative.question: In a gas, how do the molecules behave?
options: They move fast and spread out
summative.explanation: In a gas, molecules move fast and spread out to fill the space.

summative.question: What is it called when a gas changes into a liquid?
summative.explanation: Condensation is when a gas changes into a liquid.

summative.question: What is it called when a liquid changes into a gas?
summative.explanation: Evaporation is when a liquid changes into a gas.

summative.question: Which is an example of a solid?
summative.explanation: A rock is a solid because it keeps its own shape.

summative.question: Which compares solids and gases correctly?
options: Solid molecules are tightly packed; gas molecules spread out
Both have molecules that cannot move
Gas keeps its shape; solid spreads out
summative.explanation: Solid molecules are packed tightly while gas molecules spread out.

summative.question: Water droplets forming on a cold glass is an example of what?
summative.explanation: Droplets on a cold glass form by condensation as gas turns to liquid.

summative.question: What is the term for the tiny particles that make up matter?
summative.explanation: Molecules are the tiny particles that make up all matter.

summative.question: What is the amount of matter in an object called?
summative.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter in an object.

summative.question: What is the amount of space an object takes up called?
summative.explanation: Volume is the amount of space an object takes up.

summative.question: Density compares an object's mass to its what?
summative.explanation: Density compares how much mass is packed into a certain volume.

summative.question: Which property tells how easily an object can be scratched?
summative.explanation: Hardness tells how easily an object can be scratched or dented.

summative.question: Which property lets some metals be pulled by a magnet?
summative.explanation: Magnetism is the property that lets some metals be pulled by a magnet.

summative.question: What is solubility?
options: How well something dissolves in a liquid
summative.explanation: Solubility is how well a material dissolves in a liquid.

summative.question: Which is an example of using magnetism?
summative.explanation: A magnet picking up nails is an example of magnetism.

summative.question: Which compares mass and volume correctly?
options: Mass is the amount of matter; volume is the space it takes up
Mass is space; volume is matter
summative.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter while volume is the space it takes up.

summative.question: Sugar dissolving in water is an example of which property?
summative.explanation: Sugar dissolving in water shows solubility.

summative.question: A physical property is something you can observe without doing what?
options: Changing it into a new substance
summative.explanation: A physical property can be observed without changing the material into something new.

summative.question: A change that does not make a new substance is called what?
summative.explanation: A physical change does not make a new substance.

summative.question: A change that makes a brand new substance is called what?
summative.explanation: A chemical change makes a brand new substance.

summative.question: Which is an example of a physical change?
summative.explanation: Cutting paper is a physical change because no new substance forms.

summative.question: Which is an example of a chemical change?
summative.explanation: Burning wood is a chemical change because new substances form.

summative.question: A change that can be undone is called what?
summative.explanation: A reversible change can be undone, like melting and freezing water.

summative.question: A change that cannot be easily undone is called what?
summative.explanation: An irreversible change cannot be easily undone, like burning.

summative.question: Which is a clue that a chemical change happened?
options: A new color, smell, gas, or heat appears
summative.explanation: New color, smell, gas, or heat are clues of a chemical change.

summative.question: Which compares physical and chemical changes correctly?
options: Physical change keeps the same substance; chemical change makes a new one
Physical change makes a new substance; chemical change keeps the same
Both always make a new substance
summative.explanation: A physical change keeps the same substance; a chemical change makes a new one.

summative.question: Melting ice into water is which kind of change?
summative.explanation: Melting ice is a physical change that can be reversed by freezing.

summative.question: Which is an example of an irreversible change?
summative.explanation: Baking a cake is irreversible; you cannot turn it back into batter.

summative.question: What is formed when two or more materials are combined but not joined chemically?
summative.explanation: A mixture is two or more materials combined without a chemical change.

summative.question: What is a solution?
options: A mixture where one material fully dissolves in another
summative.explanation: A solution is a mixture where one material fully dissolves in another.

summative.question: In salt water, what is the solute?
summative.explanation: In salt water, the salt is the solute because it dissolves.

summative.question: In salt water, what is the solvent?
summative.explanation: In salt water, the water is the solvent that dissolves the salt.

summative.question: Which method separates a solid from a liquid using paper or cloth?
summative.explanation: Filtering separates a solid from a liquid using paper or cloth.

summative.question: Which method can separate salt from salt water?
summative.explanation: Evaporation removes the water and leaves the salt behind.

summative.question: Which is an example of a mixture?
options: A single drop of pure water
summative.explanation: A bowl of fruit salad is a mixture of different foods.

summative.question: Which compares a solute and a solvent correctly?
options: The solute dissolves; the solvent does the dissolving
The solvent dissolves; the solute does the dissolving
summative.explanation: The solute is the part that dissolves; the solvent does the dissolving.

summative.question: Which method would separate iron bits from sand?
summative.explanation: A magnet can pull out iron bits from sand.

summative.question: Which compares a mixture and a solution correctly?
options: In a solution the material fully dissolves; in many mixtures it does not
summative.explanation: In a solution the material fully dissolves, while in many mixtures parts can still be seen.

science.title: Lesson 3: Force, Motion, and Energy
science.description: Discover the forces and energy that make things move.

topics.name: Types of Forces
topics.description: Explore the pushes and pulls around us.

content.introduction: A force is a push or a pull. Forces make things move, stop, or change direction. There are several kinds of forces around us every day.

sections.title: Gravity and Friction
sections.body: Gravity pulls objects toward the Earth. Friction slows things down when two surfaces rub together.

sections.title: Magnetic Force
sections.body: Magnetic force pulls or pushes certain metals like iron, even without touching them.

sections.title: Applied Force
sections.body: An applied force is the push or pull you give an object yourself, like pushing a door.

definition.meaning: A push or a pull on an object.

content.keyTakeaway: Forces are pushes and pulls, including gravity, friction, magnetic, and applied force.
content.importantNote: More than one force can act on an object at the same time.

quiz.question: What force pulls objects toward the Earth?
quiz.explanation: Gravity is the force that pulls objects toward the Earth.

quiz.question: What force slows things down when two surfaces rub together?
quiz.explanation: Friction is the force that slows things down when surfaces rub together.

quiz.question: What force pulls or pushes certain metals like iron?
quiz.explanation: Magnetic force pulls or pushes certain metals like iron.

quiz.question: What is the force called when you push or pull an object yourself?
quiz.explanation: Applied force is the force you use when you push or pull something.

quiz.question: Which is an example of friction?
options: Rubbing your hands to warm them
quiz.explanation: Rubbing your hands to warm them is an example of friction.

quiz.question: Which is an example of gravity?
options: An apple falling from a tree
quiz.explanation: An apple falling from a tree shows gravity pulling it down.

quiz.question: What is a force?
quiz.explanation: A force is a push or a pull on an object.

quiz.question: Which compares gravity and friction correctly?
options: Gravity pulls things down; friction slows moving things
Gravity slows things; friction pulls things down
quiz.explanation: Gravity pulls things down while friction slows moving things.

quiz.question: Which is an example of magnetic force?
options: A magnet sticking to a fridge
A box sliding to a stop
quiz.explanation: A magnet sticking to a fridge is an example of magnetic force.

quiz.question: Which is an example of applied force?
options: A door opening when you push it
quiz.explanation: Pushing a door open is an example of applied force.

topics.name: Motion
topics.description: Learn how we describe moving objects.

content.introduction: Motion is a change in an object's position. We use speed, velocity, and reference points to describe how things move.

sections.title: Speed
sections.body: Speed is how fast something moves. We find speed by dividing distance by time.

sections.title: Velocity and Reference Points
sections.body: Velocity is speed in a certain direction. A reference point is a fixed spot we use to tell if something is moving.

sections.title: Acceleration
sections.body: Acceleration is a change in speed over time, like a car speeding up.

definition.meaning: How fast something moves, found by dividing distance by time.

content.keyTakeaway: Motion is described using speed, velocity, reference points, and acceleration.
content.importantNote: Speed equals distance divided by time, such as 100 meters in 10 seconds equals 10 meters per second.

quiz.question: Speed is found by dividing distance by what?
quiz.explanation: Speed equals distance divided by time.

quiz.question: If a car travels 100 meters in 10 seconds, what is its speed?
quiz.explanation: Speed = distance / time = 100 / 10 = 10 meters per second.

quiz.question: What is velocity?
quiz.explanation: Velocity is speed in a certain direction.

quiz.question: What do we call the fixed spot used to tell if something is moving?
quiz.explanation: A reference point is a fixed spot used to tell if something is moving.

quiz.question: What is acceleration?
options: A change in speed over time
quiz.explanation: Acceleration is a change in speed over time.

quiz.question: If a runner goes 50 meters in 5 seconds, what is the speed?
quiz.explanation: Speed = 50 / 5 = 10 meters per second.

quiz.question: Which is an example of acceleration?
quiz.explanation: A car speeding up is an example of acceleration.

quiz.question: Which compares speed and velocity correctly?
options: Speed tells how fast; velocity tells how fast and which direction
Velocity tells only how fast; speed tells direction
quiz.explanation: Speed tells how fast; velocity tells how fast and in which direction.

quiz.question: Why do we need a reference point?
options: To know if and how something is moving
quiz.explanation: A reference point helps us know if and how something is moving.

quiz.question: Motion means a change in an object's what?
quiz.explanation: Motion is a change in an object's position over time.

topics.name: Forms of Energy
topics.description: Discover the many forms of energy.

content.introduction: Energy is the ability to do work or make things happen. Energy comes in many forms, and it can change from one form to another.

sections.title: Kinetic and Potential
sections.body: Kinetic energy is the energy of moving things. Potential energy is stored energy waiting to be used.

sections.title: Chemical Energy
sections.body: Chemical energy is stored in food and batteries. It can change into other forms of energy.

sections.title: Sound and Electrical
sections.body: Sound energy is the energy we hear. Electrical energy powers lamps, fans, and televisions.

definition.meaning: The energy of moving objects.

content.keyTakeaway: Energy comes in forms like kinetic, potential, chemical, sound, and electrical.
content.importantNote: Energy can change from one form to another, like chemical energy in a battery becoming electrical energy.

quiz.question: What is the energy of moving objects called?
quiz.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of moving objects.

quiz.question: What is stored energy waiting to be used called?
quiz.explanation: Potential energy is stored energy waiting to be used.

quiz.question: What kind of energy is stored in food and batteries?
quiz.explanation: Chemical energy is stored in food and batteries.

quiz.question: What kind of energy do we hear?
quiz.explanation: Sound energy is the energy we hear.

quiz.question: What kind of energy powers lamps and televisions?
quiz.explanation: Electrical energy powers lamps, televisions, and other devices.

quiz.question: Which is an example of kinetic energy?
options: A book on a high shelf
quiz.explanation: A rolling ball has kinetic energy because it is moving.

quiz.question: Which is an example of potential energy?
options: A ball at the top of a hill
quiz.explanation: A ball at the top of a hill has stored potential energy.

quiz.question: Which compares kinetic and potential energy correctly?
options: Kinetic is energy of motion; potential is stored energy
Potential is energy of motion; kinetic is stored energy
quiz.explanation: Kinetic energy is energy of motion; potential energy is stored energy.

quiz.question: A battery in a toy stores which kind of energy?
quiz.explanation: A battery stores chemical energy that can become electrical energy.

quiz.question: Which is an example of sound energy?
quiz.explanation: A drum being hit makes sound energy that we can hear.

topics.name: Heat Energy
topics.description: Learn how heat moves around us.

content.introduction: Heat energy moves from hotter places to cooler places. There are three main ways heat can travel from one place to another.

sections.title: Conduction and Convection
sections.body: Conduction is heat moving through solids by direct touch. Convection is heat moving through liquids and gases as they move around.

sections.title: Radiation
sections.body: Radiation is heat that travels through empty space, like the Sun's heat reaching Earth.

sections.title: Measuring Heat
sections.body: Temperature tells how hot or cold something is. We use a thermometer to measure it.

definition.meaning: Heat moving through solids by direct touch.

content.keyTakeaway: Heat travels by conduction, convection, and radiation, and we measure it with a thermometer.
content.importantNote: Heat always moves from hotter places to cooler places.

quiz.question: How does heat travel through solids by direct touch?
quiz.explanation: Conduction is how heat travels through solids by direct touch.

quiz.question: How does heat travel through liquids and gases as they move around?
quiz.explanation: Convection is how heat travels through moving liquids and gases.

quiz.question: How does heat from the Sun reach the Earth through empty space?
quiz.explanation: Radiation lets heat from the Sun travel through empty space.

quiz.question: What measures how hot or cold something is?
quiz.explanation: Temperature measures how hot or cold something is.

quiz.question: Which tool is used to measure temperature?
quiz.explanation: A thermometer is the tool used to measure temperature.

quiz.question: Which is an example of conduction?
options: A metal spoon getting hot in soup
quiz.explanation: A metal spoon getting hot in soup is an example of conduction.

quiz.question: Which is an example of radiation?
options: Warm water rising in a pot
quiz.explanation: Feeling the Sun's warmth is an example of radiation.

quiz.question: Which compares conduction and radiation correctly?
options: Conduction needs touch; radiation can cross empty space
Radiation needs touch; conduction crosses empty space
quiz.explanation: Conduction needs direct touch while radiation can cross empty space.

quiz.question: Heat energy moves from where to where?
options: From hotter places to cooler places
quiz.explanation: Heat energy moves from hotter places to cooler places.

quiz.question: Which is an example of convection?
options: Warm water rising in a boiling pot
quiz.explanation: Warm water rising in a boiling pot is an example of convection.

topics.name: Light Energy
topics.description: Explore how light behaves.

content.introduction: Light is a form of energy that helps us see. Light can bounce, bend, or be taken in, and it can be split into many colors.

sections.title: Reflection and Refraction
sections.body: Reflection is when light bounces off a surface, like a mirror. Refraction is when light bends as it passes through water or glass.

sections.title: Absorption
sections.body: Absorption is when a surface takes in light instead of bouncing it. Dark colors absorb more light and feel warmer.

sections.title: Prism and Colors
sections.body: A prism can split white light into a spectrum of seven colors, like a rainbow.

definition.meaning: When light bounces off a surface.

content.keyTakeaway: Light can reflect, refract, or be absorbed, and a prism splits it into a spectrum of colors.
content.importantNote: A rainbow shows the spectrum of seven colors hidden inside white light.

quiz.question: What is it called when light bounces off a surface?
quiz.explanation: Reflection is when light bounces off a surface.

quiz.question: What is it called when light bends as it passes through water or glass?
quiz.explanation: Refraction is when light bends as it passes through water or glass.

quiz.question: What is it called when a surface takes in light instead of bouncing it?
quiz.explanation: Absorption is when a surface takes in light instead of bouncing it.

quiz.question: Which tool can split white light into many colors?
quiz.explanation: A prism can split white light into many colors.

quiz.question: The band of colors made when light is split is called what?
quiz.explanation: The band of colors made when light is split is called a spectrum.

quiz.question: Which is an example of reflection?
options: A straw looking bent in water
quiz.explanation: Seeing yourself in a mirror is an example of reflection.

quiz.question: Which is an example of refraction?
options: A straw looking bent in a glass of water
quiz.explanation: A straw looking bent in water is an example of refraction.

quiz.question: Which compares reflection and refraction correctly?
options: Reflection bounces light; refraction bends light
Refraction bounces light; reflection bends light
quiz.explanation: Reflection bounces light while refraction bends light.

quiz.question: Why does a black shirt feel warm in the sun?
quiz.explanation: A black shirt feels warm because it absorbs light and turns it into heat.

quiz.question: How many main colors are usually seen in a rainbow spectrum?
quiz.explanation: A rainbow spectrum usually shows seven main colors.

science.title: Lesson 4: Earth and Space
science.description: Travel through our planet and into outer space.

topics.name: Layers of the Earth
topics.description: Travel from the surface to the center of the Earth.

content.introduction: The Earth is made of layers, like a giant ball with different parts inside. There are four main layers, each one different from the others.

sections.title: Crust and Mantle
sections.body: The crust is the thin outer layer we live on. Below it is the thick mantle.

sections.title: The Core
sections.body: The outer core is made of liquid metal. The inner core is the solid, hottest center of the Earth.

sections.title: From Outside In
sections.body: The order from outside to center is crust, mantle, outer core, and inner core.

definition.meaning: The thin, outer layer of the Earth that we live on.

content.keyTakeaway: The Earth has four layers: crust, mantle, outer core, and inner core.
content.importantNote: The deeper you go inside the Earth, the hotter it becomes.

quiz.question: What is the thin, outer layer of the Earth called?
quiz.explanation: The crust is the thin, outer layer of the Earth that we live on.

quiz.question: Which thick layer lies just below the crust?
quiz.explanation: The mantle is the thick layer just below the crust.

quiz.question: Which layer is made of liquid metal?
quiz.explanation: The outer core is made of liquid metal.

quiz.question: Which is the hottest, solid center of the Earth?
quiz.explanation: The inner core is the hottest, solid center of the Earth.

quiz.question: Which is the correct order from the outside to the center of the Earth?
options: Crust, mantle, outer core, inner core
Inner core, outer core, mantle, crust
Mantle, crust, inner core, outer core
Crust, outer core, mantle, inner core
quiz.explanation: From outside to center: crust, mantle, outer core, inner core.

quiz.question: Which layer do plants and animals live on?
quiz.explanation: Living things live on the crust, the outer layer of the Earth.

quiz.question: Which compares the crust and the inner core correctly?
options: The crust is thin and outer; the inner core is solid and at the center
The crust is the center; the inner core is the outer layer
quiz.explanation: The crust is the thin outer layer; the inner core is the solid center.

quiz.question: What is the inner core mostly made of?
quiz.explanation: The inner core is mostly made of solid metal.

quiz.question: Which compares the outer core and the inner core correctly?
options: The outer core is liquid; the inner core is solid
The outer core is solid; the inner core is liquid
quiz.explanation: The outer core is liquid metal while the inner core is solid metal.

quiz.question: How many main layers does the Earth have?
quiz.explanation: The Earth has four main layers: crust, mantle, outer core, and inner core.

topics.name: Weather and Climate
topics.description: Learn the difference between weather and climate.

content.introduction: Weather and climate both describe the air and sky, but they are not the same. Weather changes quickly, while climate is the pattern over many years.

sections.title: Weather
sections.body: Weather is the day-to-day state of the air, like rain, wind, temperature, and sunshine.

sections.title: Climate
sections.body: Climate is the usual weather of a place over many years.

sections.title: Seasons
sections.body: Seasons happen because the Earth is tilted as it goes around the Sun.

definition.meaning: The usual weather of a place over many years.

content.keyTakeaway: Weather changes day to day, while climate is the pattern over many years.
content.importantNote: We can measure weather using temperature, rain, and wind.

quiz.question: What is the day-to-day state of the air, like rain or sunshine, called?
quiz.explanation: Weather is the day-to-day state of the air, like rain or sunshine.

quiz.question: What is the usual weather of a place over many years called?
quiz.explanation: Climate is the usual weather of a place over many years.

quiz.question: What is moving air called?
quiz.explanation: Wind is moving air.

quiz.question: What measures how hot or cold the air is?
quiz.explanation: Temperature measures how hot or cold the air is.

quiz.question: What falls from clouds as drops of water?
quiz.explanation: Rain falls from clouds as drops of water.

quiz.question: Which is an example of weather?
options: This city is usually hot all year
quiz.explanation: Saying it is raining today describes weather.

quiz.question: Which is an example of climate?
options: This place is usually rainy for many years
quiz.explanation: Describing the usual weather over many years is climate.

quiz.question: Which compares weather and climate correctly?
options: Weather changes day to day; climate is the pattern over many years
Climate changes day to day; weather lasts many years
Both mean the same exact thing
quiz.explanation: Weather changes day to day; climate is the pattern over many years.

quiz.question: What causes the different seasons?
options: The Earth's tilt as it goes around the Sun
quiz.explanation: Seasons are caused by the Earth's tilt as it orbits the Sun.

quiz.question: Which of these is a type of weather you can measure?
quiz.explanation: Temperature is a type of weather you can measure.

topics.name: Solar System
topics.description: Tour the Sun and the eight planets.

content.introduction: Our Solar System has the Sun at the center and eight planets that move around it. The planets are different in size and what they are made of.

sections.title: The Sun and Planets
sections.body: The Sun is a star at the center. The eight planets, in order, are Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune.

sections.title: Inner and Outer Planets
sections.body: The inner planets are small and rocky. The outer planets are large gas giants.

sections.title: What Keeps Them Moving
sections.body: The Sun's gravity keeps the planets moving around it.

definition.meaning: The Sun and the eight planets that move around it.

content.keyTakeaway: The Solar System has the Sun and eight planets, from rocky inner planets to gas giants.
content.importantNote: Earth is the third planet from the Sun and the only one known to have life.

quiz.question: How many planets are in our Solar System?
quiz.explanation: There are eight planets in our Solar System.

quiz.question: Which is the star at the center of the Solar System?
quiz.explanation: The Sun is the star at the center of the Solar System.

quiz.question: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
quiz.explanation: Mercury is the planet closest to the Sun.

quiz.question: Which is the correct order of the first four planets from the Sun?
quiz.explanation: The first four planets are Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars.

quiz.question: Which planet do we live on?
quiz.explanation: We live on Earth, the third planet from the Sun.

quiz.question: Which is the largest planet, a gas giant?
quiz.explanation: Jupiter is the largest planet and a gas giant.

quiz.question: Which compares inner and outer planets correctly?
options: Inner planets are small and rocky; outer planets are large gas giants
Inner planets are gas giants; outer planets are rocky
All planets are the same size
Outer planets are closest to the Sun
quiz.explanation: Inner planets are small and rocky while outer planets are large gas giants.

quiz.question: Which of these is a rocky inner planet?
quiz.explanation: Mars is a rocky inner planet.

quiz.question: Which planet is famous for its bright rings?
quiz.explanation: Saturn is famous for its bright rings.

quiz.question: What keeps the planets moving around the Sun?
quiz.explanation: The Sun's gravity keeps the planets moving around it.

topics.name: Phases of the Moon
topics.description: Discover why the Moon changes shape.

content.introduction: The Moon seems to change shape during the month. These shapes are called phases. The Moon does not really change; we just see different lit parts.

sections.title: New Moon and Full Moon
sections.body: During a new moon, we cannot see the lit Moon. During a full moon, we see the whole lit side.

sections.title: Waxing and Waning
sections.body: Waxing means the lit part is growing. Waning means the lit part is shrinking.

sections.title: The Lunar Cycle
sections.body: The full cycle of Moon phases takes about 29.5 days.

definition.meaning: The changing shapes of the Moon that we see during the month.

content.keyTakeaway: The Moon's phases go from new moon to full moon and back in about 29.5 days.
content.importantNote: The Moon does not make its own light; it reflects light from the Sun.

quiz.question: What is the Moon phase called when we cannot see the lit Moon?
quiz.explanation: During a new moon, we cannot see the lit part of the Moon.

quiz.question: What is the phase when we see the whole lit Moon?
quiz.explanation: During a full moon, we see the whole lit side of the Moon.

quiz.question: What does 'waxing' mean for the Moon?
quiz.explanation: Waxing means the lit part of the Moon is growing larger.

quiz.question: What does 'waning' mean for the Moon?
quiz.explanation: Waning means the lit part of the Moon is shrinking.

quiz.question: About how many days does the full lunar cycle take?
quiz.explanation: The full lunar cycle takes about 29.5 days.

quiz.question: Which is a thin, growing sliver of the Moon?
quiz.explanation: A waxing crescent is a thin, growing sliver of the Moon.

quiz.question: Why does the Moon seem to change shape?
options: Because we see different lit parts as it orbits Earth
quiz.explanation: The Moon seems to change shape because we see different lit parts as it orbits Earth.

quiz.question: Which compares a new moon and a full moon correctly?
options: A new moon shows no lit part; a full moon shows the whole lit part
A new moon shows the whole lit part; a full moon shows none
Both happen only once a year
quiz.explanation: A new moon shows no lit part while a full moon shows the whole lit part.

quiz.question: What is the order after a new moon?
options: Full moon then new moon instantly
quiz.explanation: After a new moon, the Moon waxes toward a full moon.

quiz.question: The changing shapes of the Moon are called its what?
quiz.explanation: The changing shapes of the Moon are called its phases.

summative.question: What is the thin, outer layer of the Earth called?
summative.explanation: The crust is the thin, outer layer of the Earth that we live on.

summative.question: Which thick layer lies just below the crust?
summative.explanation: The mantle is the thick layer just below the crust.

summative.question: Which layer is made of liquid metal?
summative.explanation: The outer core is made of liquid metal.

summative.question: Which is the hottest, solid center of the Earth?
summative.explanation: The inner core is the hottest, solid center of the Earth.

summative.question: Which is the correct order from the outside to the center of the Earth?
options: Crust, mantle, outer core, inner core
Inner core, outer core, mantle, crust
Mantle, crust, inner core, outer core
Crust, outer core, mantle, inner core
summative.explanation: From outside to center: crust, mantle, outer core, inner core.

summative.question: Which layer do plants and animals live on?
summative.explanation: Living things live on the crust, the outer layer of the Earth.

summative.question: Which compares the crust and the inner core correctly?
options: The crust is thin and outer; the inner core is solid and at the center
The crust is the center; the inner core is the outer layer
summative.explanation: The crust is the thin outer layer; the inner core is the solid center.

summative.question: What is the inner core mostly made of?
summative.explanation: The inner core is mostly made of solid metal.

summative.question: Which compares the outer core and the inner core correctly?
options: The outer core is liquid; the inner core is solid
The outer core is solid; the inner core is liquid
summative.explanation: The outer core is liquid metal while the inner core is solid metal.

summative.question: How many main layers does the Earth have?
summative.explanation: The Earth has four main layers: crust, mantle, outer core, and inner core.

summative.question: What is the day-to-day state of the air, like rain or sunshine, called?
summative.explanation: Weather is the day-to-day state of the air, like rain or sunshine.

summative.question: What is the usual weather of a place over many years called?
summative.explanation: Climate is the usual weather of a place over many years.

summative.question: What is moving air called?
summative.explanation: Wind is moving air.

summative.question: What measures how hot or cold the air is?
summative.explanation: Temperature measures how hot or cold the air is.

summative.question: What falls from clouds as drops of water?
summative.explanation: Rain falls from clouds as drops of water.

summative.question: Which is an example of weather?
options: This city is usually hot all year
summative.explanation: Saying it is raining today describes weather.

summative.question: Which is an example of climate?
options: This place is usually rainy for many years
summative.explanation: Describing the usual weather over many years is climate.

summative.question: Which compares weather and climate correctly?
options: Weather changes day to day; climate is the pattern over many years
Climate changes day to day; weather lasts many years
Both mean the same exact thing
summative.explanation: Weather changes day to day; climate is the pattern over many years.

summative.question: What causes the different seasons?
options: The Earth's tilt as it goes around the Sun
summative.explanation: Seasons are caused by the Earth's tilt as it orbits the Sun.

summative.question: Which of these is a type of weather you can measure?
summative.explanation: Temperature is a type of weather you can measure.

science.title: Lesson 5: Living Things and Ecosystems
science.description: Learn about living things and how they share the Earth.

topics.name: Plants and Animals
topics.description: Learn how plants and animals get food.

content.introduction: Plants and animals are living things made of cells. Plants make their own food, while animals must eat to get energy.

sections.title: Photosynthesis
sections.body: Plants make their own food through photosynthesis, using sunlight, water, and air.

sections.title: Chlorophyll
sections.body: Chlorophyll is the green substance in plants that captures sunlight for photosynthesis.

sections.title: Producers and Consumers
sections.body: Plants are producers because they make food. Animals are consumers because they eat other living things. All living things are made of tiny cells.

definition.meaning: The process plants use to make their own food.

content.keyTakeaway: Plants are producers that make food by photosynthesis, while animals are consumers.
content.importantNote: All living things, both plants and animals, are made of tiny cells.

quiz.question: What is the process plants use to make their own food called?
quiz.explanation: Photosynthesis is the process plants use to make their own food.

quiz.question: What green substance in plants helps them make food?
quiz.explanation: Chlorophyll is the green substance that helps plants make food.

quiz.question: Living things that make their own food are called what?
quiz.explanation: Producers are living things, like plants, that make their own food.

quiz.question: Living things that eat other living things for food are called what?
quiz.explanation: Consumers are living things that eat other living things.

quiz.question: What is the smallest building block of living things called?
quiz.explanation: A cell is the smallest building block of living things.

quiz.question: Which is an example of a producer?
quiz.explanation: A green plant is a producer because it makes its own food.

quiz.question: Which is an example of a consumer?
quiz.explanation: A deer eating grass is a consumer.

quiz.question: Which compares producers and consumers correctly?
options: Producers make their own food; consumers eat other living things
Consumers make their own food; producers eat others
quiz.explanation: Producers make their own food while consumers eat other living things.

quiz.question: What does a plant need for photosynthesis?
quiz.explanation: A plant needs sunlight, water, and air for photosynthesis.

quiz.question: Why is chlorophyll important?
options: It captures sunlight so plants can make food
quiz.explanation: Chlorophyll captures sunlight so plants can make food.

topics.name: Food Chains
topics.description: Follow energy from the Sun to living things.

content.introduction: A food chain shows how energy moves from one living thing to another. It usually starts with the Sun and ends with decomposers.

sections.title: Start with the Sun
sections.body: Energy starts with the Sun. Producers, like plants, use the Sun to make food.

sections.title: Consumers
sections.body: A primary consumer eats producers. A secondary consumer eats primary consumers.

sections.title: Decomposers
sections.body: Decomposers break down dead plants and animals and return nutrients to the soil.

definition.meaning: A path that shows how energy moves from one living thing to another.

content.keyTakeaway: A food chain goes from the Sun to producers, consumers, and then decomposers.
content.importantNote: Decomposers are important because they return nutrients to the soil.

quiz.question: Where does the energy in most food chains start?
quiz.explanation: The energy in most food chains starts with the Sun.

quiz.question: In a food chain, what comes right after the Sun?
quiz.explanation: After the Sun comes the producer, like a plant.

quiz.question: What is an animal that eats a producer called?
quiz.explanation: A primary consumer is an animal that eats a producer.

quiz.question: What eats a primary consumer?
quiz.explanation: A secondary consumer eats a primary consumer.

quiz.question: What breaks down dead plants and animals?
quiz.explanation: Decomposers break down dead plants and animals.

quiz.question: Which is the correct order in a simple food chain?
options: Sun, producer, primary consumer, secondary consumer
Producer, Sun, secondary consumer, primary consumer
Secondary consumer, primary consumer, producer, Sun
quiz.explanation: A simple food chain goes Sun, producer, primary consumer, secondary consumer.

quiz.question: Which is an example of a primary consumer?
options: A fungus on a dead log
quiz.explanation: A rabbit eating grass is a primary consumer.

quiz.question: Which is an example of a decomposer?
options: A mushroom growing on a dead log
quiz.explanation: A mushroom growing on a dead log is a decomposer.

quiz.question: Which compares a producer and a decomposer correctly?
options: A producer makes food; a decomposer breaks down dead things
A decomposer makes food; a producer breaks down dead things
Both make their own food only
quiz.explanation: A producer makes food while a decomposer breaks down dead things.

quiz.question: Why are decomposers important?
options: They return nutrients to the soil
quiz.explanation: Decomposers return nutrients to the soil so plants can grow.

topics.name: Habitats
topics.description: Explore the homes of living things.

content.introduction: A habitat is the natural home of a plant or animal. Different habitats have different conditions, and living things adapt to survive in them.

sections.title: Different Habitats
sections.body: Some habitats are the desert, rainforest, tundra, ocean, and grassland. Each one is different.

sections.title: Conditions
sections.body: Deserts are dry, rainforests are wet, the tundra is cold, the ocean is salty, and grasslands are covered with grass.

sections.title: Adaptations
sections.body: An adaptation is a special feature that helps a living thing survive in its habitat, like a camel's hump in the desert.

definition.meaning: The natural home of a plant or animal.

content.keyTakeaway: Habitats like deserts and rainforests are different, and living things adapt to survive in them.
content.importantNote: Adaptations help living things survive in their special habitats.

quiz.question: What is the natural home of a plant or animal called?
quiz.explanation: A habitat is the natural home of a plant or animal.

quiz.question: Which habitat is very dry with little rain?
quiz.explanation: A desert is a very dry habitat with little rain.

quiz.question: Which habitat is warm, wet, and full of tall trees?
quiz.explanation: A rainforest is warm, wet, and full of tall trees.

quiz.question: Which habitat is very cold with frozen ground?
quiz.explanation: A tundra is a very cold habitat with frozen ground.

quiz.question: Which habitat is full of salt water?
quiz.explanation: The ocean is a habitat full of salt water.

quiz.question: A camel storing fat in its hump is an adaptation for which habitat?
quiz.explanation: A camel's hump helps it survive in the dry desert.

quiz.question: Which habitat is covered with grass and few trees?
quiz.explanation: A grassland is covered with grass and has few trees.

quiz.question: Which compares a desert and a rainforest correctly?
options: A desert is dry; a rainforest is wet
A desert is wet; a rainforest is dry
Both are full of salt water
quiz.explanation: A desert is dry while a rainforest is wet.

quiz.question: What is an adaptation?
options: A special feature that helps a living thing survive in its habitat
quiz.explanation: An adaptation is a special feature that helps a living thing survive.

quiz.question: A thick fur coat is an adaptation that helps animals live in which habitat?
quiz.explanation: Thick fur helps animals stay warm in the cold tundra.

topics.name: Ecosystems
topics.description: Learn how living and non-living things work together.

content.introduction: An ecosystem is all the living and non-living things in an area working together. Each part has a job, and the parts depend on each other.

sections.title: Biotic and Abiotic
sections.body: Biotic factors are the living parts, like plants and animals. Abiotic factors are the non-living parts, like sunlight, water, and rocks.

sections.title: Balance
sections.body: An ecosystem is in balance when living things have what they need and no group grows too large.

sections.title: Interdependence
sections.body: Interdependence means living things depend on each other to survive. Removing one part can upset the balance.

definition.meaning: All the living and non-living things in an area working together.

content.keyTakeaway: Ecosystems have biotic and abiotic factors that depend on each other to stay in balance.
content.importantNote: Even small changes in an ecosystem can affect many living things.

quiz.question: What do we call all the living and non-living things in an area working together?
quiz.explanation: An ecosystem is all the living and non-living things in an area working together.

quiz.question: What are the living parts of an ecosystem called?
quiz.explanation: Biotic factors are the living parts of an ecosystem.

quiz.question: What are the non-living parts of an ecosystem called?
quiz.explanation: Abiotic factors are the non-living parts of an ecosystem.

quiz.question: Which is an example of a biotic factor?
quiz.explanation: A fish is a biotic (living) factor.

quiz.question: Which is an example of an abiotic factor?
quiz.explanation: Sunlight is an abiotic (non-living) factor.

quiz.question: What does it mean for an ecosystem to be in balance?
options: Living things have what they need and no group grows too large
There is no water at all
quiz.explanation: Balance means living things have what they need and no group grows too large.

quiz.question: What is interdependence in an ecosystem?
options: Living things depending on each other to survive
Living things never needing each other
quiz.explanation: Interdependence means living things depend on each other to survive.

quiz.question: Which compares biotic and abiotic factors correctly?
options: Biotic factors are living; abiotic factors are non-living
Biotic factors are non-living; abiotic factors are living
quiz.explanation: Biotic factors are living while abiotic factors are non-living.

quiz.question: What can happen if one part of an ecosystem is removed?
options: The balance can be upset and other living things are affected
quiz.explanation: Removing one part can upset the balance and affect other living things.

quiz.question: Why is balance important in an ecosystem?
options: So all living things can keep getting what they need
quiz.explanation: Balance lets all living things keep getting what they need.

topics.name: Environmental Conservation
topics.description: Discover how to take care of the Earth.

content.introduction: Conservation means protecting and caring for the Earth and its living things. We can all help by making good choices every day.

sections.title: The 3 R's
sections.body: We can reduce by using less, reuse by using things again, and recycle by making old materials into new things.

sections.title: Deforestation
sections.body: Deforestation is the cutting down of many trees. It destroys habitats and can harm wildlife.

sections.title: Endangered Species
sections.body: Endangered species are animals close to dying out. We can help by protecting their habitats and not littering.

definition.meaning: To turn old materials into new things.

content.keyTakeaway: Conservation means caring for the Earth using the 3 R's and protecting habitats and species.
content.importantNote: Every small action, like not littering, helps protect our planet.

quiz.question: What do the 3 R's stand for?
quiz.explanation: The 3 R's stand for Reduce, Reuse, and Recycle.

quiz.question: What does 'reduce' mean?
options: Use less so there is less waste
quiz.explanation: Reduce means to use less so there is less waste.

quiz.question: What does 'reuse' mean?
options: Use something again instead of throwing it away
quiz.explanation: Reuse means to use something again instead of throwing it away.

quiz.question: What does 'recycle' mean?
options: Turn old materials into new things
quiz.explanation: Recycle means to turn old materials into new things.

quiz.question: What is the cutting down of many trees called?
quiz.explanation: Deforestation is the cutting down of many trees.

quiz.question: What are animals that are close to dying out completely called?
quiz.explanation: Endangered species are animals close to dying out completely.

quiz.question: Which is an example of reusing?
options: Using a bottle again as a water container
Throwing a bottle in the river
quiz.explanation: Using a bottle again as a container is an example of reusing.

quiz.question: Which compares reduce and recycle correctly?
options: Reduce means use less; recycle means make new things from old materials
Reduce means make new things; recycle means use less
quiz.explanation: Reduce means to use less while recycle means to make new things from old materials.

quiz.question: Why is deforestation harmful?
options: It destroys habitats and can harm wildlife
It has no effect at all
quiz.explanation: Deforestation destroys habitats and can harm wildlife.

quiz.question: How can students help protect endangered species?
options: By protecting habitats and not littering
quiz.explanation: Students can help by protecting habitats and not littering.

summative.explanation: Reduce means to use less so there is less waste.

summative.question: What does 'reuse' mean?
options: Use something again instead of throwing it away
summative.explanation: Reuse means to use something again instead of throwing it away.

summative.question: What does 'recycle' mean?
options: Turn old materials into new things
summative.explanation: Recycle means to turn old materials into new things.

summative.question: What is the cutting down of many trees called?
summative.explanation: Deforestation is the cutting down of many trees.

summative.question: What are animals that are close to dying out completely called?
summative.explanation: Endangered species are animals close to dying out completely.

summative.question: Which is an example of reusing?
options: Using a bottle again as a water container
Throwing a bottle in the river

summative.question: Which compares reduce and recycle correctly?

Reduce means make new things; recycle means use less
